content_en:
The chapter on muraqabah — the constant awareness that Allah sees every action, hears every word, and knows every thought — represents one of the most psychologically transformative concepts in Riyadh al-Salihin. Al-Nawawi grounds this chapter in the Quranic declaration: 'And He is with you wherever you are' (Quran 57:4), and: 'Surely Allah is ever Watchful over you' (Quran 4:1). The Quran's repeated use of the name 'al-Raqib' (the Watchful, the Observer) as one of Allah's names establishes muraqabah not merely as a spiritual practice but as a recognition of an always-present reality.

The primary hadith in this chapter is the celebrated hadith of Jibril, recorded in both al-Bukhari and Muslim, in which the Angel Jibril appeared to the Prophet ﷺ in human form and asked him about Islam, Iman, and Ihsan. The Prophet's ﷺ definition of Ihsan (spiritual excellence) is the foundation of the entire concept of muraqabah: 'Ihsan is that you worship Allah as if you see Him. And if you do not see Him, then indeed He sees you.' This definition presents two levels of spiritual awareness. The higher level — 'as if you see Him' — is the station of the gnostics and the highest of the Companions; it involves such a vivid awareness of Allah's presence that the worshiper's heart is as attentive as if they stood before Him directly. The lower but still essential level — 'He sees you' — is the awareness that even if the heart has not yet achieved that direct vivid awareness, the believer knows with certainty that they are seen, and this knowledge shapes their every action.

Al-Nawawi includes several hadiths that illustrate the practical consequences of muraqabah. The Prophet ﷺ is reported to have said: 'Guard Allah, and He will guard you.' (Al-Tirmidhi.) This condensed formulation means: be mindful of Allah's boundaries, His commands, and His presence — and He will protect you from all that would harm you. The believer who internalizes muraqabah does not need an external supervisor, an audience, or a social sanction to perform righteous acts or avoid prohibited ones — Allah's watchfulness is the sufficient motivation.

A particularly striking narration in this chapter is the story of the young man who, when offered an unlawful relationship by a young woman in a secluded place, refused with the words: 'I fear Allah.' (Al-Bukhari.) This response — in a situation of total privacy, total opportunity, and total apparent freedom from social consequence — is the practical expression of muraqabah at its most powerful. The young man's awareness that Allah sees, even in the most private moment, provided the moral architecture that no social mechanism could have supplied.

For al-Nawawi, muraqabah is not merely a spiritual ideal but the practical prerequisite for consistent righteousness. A person who only behaves well when watched by other people has a fragile and contingent morality. A person whose behavior is governed by the constant awareness of Allah's watchfulness has a morality that is unconditional, consistent, and truly Islamic. This is why the early scholars regarded muraqabah as one of the foundational stations of the spiritual path.
